report_name: Miasma Worm Breaches 73 Microsoft GitHub Repos in AI-Powered Supply Chain Attack

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: Threat group that publicly released the 'Mini Shai-Hulud' worm, of which the Miasma worm is considered an evolution. Their open-sourcing of the worm has led to its proliferation and modification by various actors.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: The threat actor utilized previously compromised credentials of a contributor to gain initial access and push malicious code to a Microsoft GitHub repository.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
attack_patterns.description: The Miasma worm's payload was designed to execute via a hook or trigger within AI coding assistants when they parsed a compromised repository's code or configuration.

attack_patterns.name: Timestomp
attack_patterns.description: The malicious commit pushed to the Azure/durabletask repository was backdated to evade simple timeline analysis and hide the malicious changes within the repository's history.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores
attack_patterns.description: The primary objective of the Miasma worm's payload was to harvest developer credentials, including API keys and tokens, from the developer's environment.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_patterns.description: The Miasma worm propagated by using stolen credentials to access and infect other GitHub repositories, constituting a software supply chain compromise.

malware.name: Miasma worm
malware.description: A self-replicating worm that compromised 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories. It propagates by harvesting developer credentials, with its payload being triggered by the use of AI-powered coding assistants.

malware.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
malware.description: An open-sourced worm created by TeamPCP, which the Miasma worm is a variant of.

malware.name: Hades
aliases: Hades - The End for the Damned
malware.description: A related malicious repository that appeared on GitHub, associated with the proliferation of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm code.

tools.name: Claude Code
tools.description: AI-powered coding assistant that acted as a trigger for the Miasma worm's malicious payload when used by a developer on a compromised repository.

tools.name: Gemini CLI
tools.description: AI-powered coding assistant that acted as a trigger for the Miasma worm's malicious payload.

tools.name: Cursor
tools.description: AI-powered coding assistant that acted as a trigger for the Miasma worm's malicious payload.

tools.name: VS Code
tools.description: AI-powered coding assistant that acted as a trigger for the Miasma worm's malicious payload.

identities.name: Microsoft
identities.description: Victim of the Miasma worm attack, with 73 repositories across four of its major GitHub organizations (Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, MicrosoftDocs) being compromised.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: The software development platform hosting the compromised repositories. GitHub staff responded by disabling the 73 affected repositories to mitigate the attack.

infrastructure.name: Compromised Microsoft GitHub Repositories
infrastructure.description: 73 GitHub repositories belonging to Microsoft that were compromised by the Miasma worm to host the malicious payload and facilitate its propagation. The initial compromise was in the Azure/durabletask repository.

indicators.name: Suspicious Backdated Git Commit
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Backdated Git Commit
id: 9c5b1a3d-8e4f-4b2a-9c1d-0f8e7d6c5b4a
status: experimental
description: Detects developers using the 'git commit --date' flag to backdate commits, which is an unusual practice and was used by the Miasma worm.
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-08
references:
  - https://www.rescana.com/post/miasma-worm-supply-chain-attack-73-microsoft-github-repositories-compromised-via-ai-coding-tools
level: medium
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate but rare use cases for backdating commits.
tags:
  - attack.t1070.006
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\git.exe'
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - 'commit'
      - '--date='
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the use of 'git commit --date', a technique used by the Miasma worm to backdate a malicious commit and evade timeline analysis.

indicators.name: Anomalous VS Code Workspace Settings Modification
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous VS Code Workspace Settings Modification
id: f4a2b1c0-9d8e-4f3a-8b7c-6d5e4f3a2b1c
status: experimental
description: Monitors for unusual modifications to VS Code workspace settings files (settings.json) that could be used to trigger malicious scripts, as seen in the Miasma worm attack.
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-08
references:
  - https://www.rescana.com/post/miasma-worm-supply-chain-attack-73-microsoft-github-repositories-compromised-via-ai-coding-tools
level: medium
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate developer changes to workspace settings.
tags:
  - attack.t1059
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: '\.vscode\settings.json'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Monitors for modifications to the '.vscode/settings.json' file, which could indicate an attempt to create a malicious execution trigger similar to the one used by the Miasma worm.

relationships.target_name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools

relationships.source_name: Anomalous VS Code Workspace Settings Modification
